remedies.name: Elderberry Syrup
preparation_steps: Harvest ripe elderberries in late summer (dark purple, not red).
Remove berries from stems using a fork.
Combine 1 cup dried elderberries (or 2 cups fresh) with 3 cups water in a saucepan.
Add 1 cinnamon stick, 4 whole cloves, and 1 tablespoon fresh grated ginger.
Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 45 minutes until liquid reduces by half.
Strain through cheesecloth, pressing berries to extract all juice.
Let cool to lukewarm, then stir in 1 cup raw honey.
Store in a glass jar in the refrigerator for up to 3 months.
remedies.safety_notes: Never consume raw elderberries, bark, or leaves -- they contain cyanogenic glycosides. Always cook berries before use. Not recommended for pregnant or nursing women without consulting a healthcare provider. May interact with immunosuppressant medications.
remedies.season: late summer to early fall (harvest), year-round (use stored syrup)

remedies.name: Chamomile Sleep Tea
preparation_steps: Harvest chamomile flowers when petals are fully open and beginning to fold back.
Dry flowers on a screen in a warm, well-ventilated area for 1-2 weeks.
Use 1 tablespoon dried flowers (or 2 tablespoons fresh) per cup.
Pour boiling water over flowers in a cup or teapot.
Cover and steep for 5-10 minutes (longer steeping increases potency).
Strain and sweeten with honey if desired.
Drink 30-60 minutes before bedtime for best sleep support.
remedies.safety_notes: Avoid if allergic to plants in the Asteraceae/daisy family. May increase effects of blood thinners and sedatives. Generally recognized as safe for most adults.

remedies.name: Plantain Leaf Poultice
preparation_steps: Identify plantain leaves in your yard (broad ribbed leaves growing in a rosette, not the banana-like fruit).
Harvest several fresh, clean leaves -- avoid areas treated with pesticides.
Wash leaves thoroughly under running water.
Chew or crush the leaves to release the plant juices (chewing works fastest in the field).
Apply the mashed leaf directly to the bug bite, sting, or minor wound.
Hold in place with a bandage or cloth wrap.
Replace with fresh poultice every 2-4 hours.
For a more prepared version, blend leaves with a small amount of water to form a paste.
remedies.safety_notes: For external use only. Clean the area before applying. Seek medical attention for severe allergic reactions, deep wounds, or signs of infection. Ensure correct plant identification.
remedies.season: spring through fall (fresh), year-round (dried leaves reconstituted)
remedies.shelf_life: use fresh; dried leaves keep 1 year

remedies.name: Dandelion Root Tea
preparation_steps: Dig dandelion roots in early spring or late fall when energy is stored in the root.
Wash roots thoroughly, scrubbing off all soil.
Chop roots into small pieces (about 1/4 inch).
Optional: roast chopped roots at 300F for 2 hours for a coffee-like flavor.
Use 1-2 tablespoons of chopped root per cup of water.
Place roots in a saucepan with cold water and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain and serve. Add honey or lemon to taste.
remedies.safety_notes: Avoid if allergic to Asteraceae family plants. May interact with lithium, antibiotics, and diuretic medications. Not recommended for people with gallbladder problems or bile duct obstruction. Harvest only from areas free of pesticides and herbicides.
remedies.season: early spring or late fall (harvest), year-round (dried root)

remedies.name: Lavender Infused Oil
preparation_steps: Harvest lavender stems when about half the flower buds have opened.
Bundle and hang upside down to dry for 1-2 weeks in a dark, well-ventilated space.
Strip dried flowers from stems and lightly crush them.
Fill a clean glass jar halfway with dried lavender flowers.
Cover completely with a carrier oil (olive oil or sweet almond oil work well).
Seal jar and place in a sunny windowsill for 3-4 weeks, shaking daily.
Strain through cheesecloth into a clean jar.
Apply to temples for headache relief, or use as a massage oil for stress.
remedies.safety_notes: For external use only. Do a patch test before first use to check for skin sensitivity. Not the same as essential oil -- this infused oil is milder and safer for direct skin application. Keep away from eyes.
remedies.shelf_life: 6-12 months stored in a cool, dark place

remedies.name: Peppermint Digestive Tea
preparation_steps: Harvest peppermint leaves just before the plant flowers for peak oil content.
Rinse leaves and pat dry, or dry on screens for 1-2 weeks for storage.
Use 5-10 fresh leaves (or 1 tablespoon dried) per cup.
Bruise fresh leaves gently between your fingers to release oils.
Pour boiling water over the leaves.
Cover and steep for 7-10 minutes.
Strain and drink warm after meals for digestive support.
Can be chilled for iced peppermint tea in summer.
remedies.safety_notes: May worsen acid reflux or GERD symptoms in some people. Not recommended for infants or very young children due to menthol content. May interact with certain medications metabolized by the liver.

remedies.name: Echinacea Tincture
preparation_steps: Harvest echinacea roots in fall after the plant is at least 3 years old, or use fresh flowers and leaves.
Wash roots thoroughly and chop into small pieces.
Fill a glass jar 1/3 to 1/2 full with chopped roots (or 2/3 with fresh aerial parts).
Cover completely with 80-proof vodka or brandy.
Seal tightly and label with date and contents.
Store in a cool, dark place for 4-6 weeks, shaking the jar daily.
Strain through cheesecloth, squeezing out all liquid.
Store tincture in dark dropper bottles. Take 1-2 dropperfuls at first sign of cold, up to 3 times daily.
remedies.safety_notes: Not recommended for people with autoimmune disorders. Do not take continuously for more than 8 weeks. May cause allergic reactions in people sensitive to Asteraceae family plants. Consult a healthcare provider if pregnant or taking immunosuppressants.

remedies.name: Ginger Compress
preparation_steps: Grate 4-5 tablespoons of fresh ginger root (do not peel if organic).
Wrap grated ginger in cheesecloth and tie into a bundle.
Bring 1 quart of water to just below boiling (do not let it boil).
Turn off heat and squeeze the ginger bundle into the hot water, letting it steep.
Dip a clean washcloth into the ginger water (use tongs -- it will be very hot).
Wring out excess liquid carefully.
Apply the warm cloth to the affected area (sore muscles, stiff joints).
Cover with a dry towel to retain heat. Replace when cloth cools. Repeat 3-4 times per session.
remedies.safety_notes: Test temperature before applying to avoid burns. Do not apply to broken skin, rashes, or inflamed areas. Discontinue if skin becomes red or irritated. Not suitable for areas with poor circulation or numbness.

remedies.name: Calendula Healing Salve
preparation_steps: Harvest calendula flowers at midday when fully open and dry.
Dry flowers completely on screens for 1-2 weeks (moisture causes mold).
Fill a jar halfway with dried calendula petals.
Cover with olive oil and let infuse for 4-6 weeks in a sunny spot, shaking daily.
Strain the infused oil through cheesecloth.
Gently melt 1 ounce beeswax per cup of infused oil in a double boiler.
Stir beeswax into the warm infused oil until fully combined.
Pour into small tins or jars. Let cool completely before capping. Apply to minor wounds, dry skin, or rashes.
remedies.safety_notes: For external use only. Avoid if allergic to Asteraceae family plants. Do not apply to deep or puncture wounds. Discontinue use if irritation occurs. Beeswax alternative: candelilla wax for vegan version.

remedies.name: Rosemary Memory Infusion
preparation_steps: Clip 3-4 sprigs of fresh rosemary (about 4-6 inches each).
Rinse gently and pat dry.
Place sprigs in a teapot or heat-safe jar.
Pour 2 cups of just-boiled water over the rosemary.
Cover and steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain and serve warm. Add honey and lemon if desired.
Drink 1-2 cups daily for cognitive support.
Alternatively, use as a hair rinse for scalp stimulation after cooling.
remedies.safety_notes: Generally safe in culinary amounts. Large medicinal doses may be problematic for people with seizure disorders, high blood pressure, or bleeding disorders. Avoid large doses during pregnancy. May interact with blood thinners and ACE inhibitors.
remedies.shelf_life: use same day; dry leaves keep 1 year

remedies.name: Thyme Steam Inhalation
preparation_steps: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil.
Remove from heat and add a large handful of fresh thyme (or 3 tablespoons dried).
Let steep for 2-3 minutes.
Place the pot on a stable surface. Sit comfortably in front of it.
Drape a towel over your head and the pot, creating a tent to trap steam.
Breathe slowly and deeply through your nose for 5-10 minutes.
Keep eyes closed to avoid irritation.
Repeat 2-3 times daily during congestion.
remedies.safety_notes: Keep face at least 12 inches from the water to prevent burns. Not suitable for young children due to burn risk. Discontinue if breathing becomes difficult. People with asthma should use with caution as steam may trigger spasms.

remedies.name: Lemon Balm Calm Tea
preparation_steps: Harvest lemon balm leaves before the plant flowers for best flavor and potency.
Use 2 tablespoons fresh leaves (or 1 tablespoon dried) per cup.
Tear or bruise fresh leaves to release aromatic oils.
Place leaves in a cup and pour just-boiled water over them.
Cover and steep for 5-10 minutes (covering preserves volatile oils).
Strain and drink warm.
Best consumed 2-3 times daily during periods of stress or anxiety.
Can be combined with chamomile for enhanced calming effect.
remedies.safety_notes: May interact with thyroid medications and sedatives. Generally recognized as safe. Avoid excessive use during pregnancy. May cause drowsiness -- do not drive or operate machinery if affected.

remedies.name: Yarrow Wound Compress
preparation_steps: Identify yarrow by its feathery, fern-like leaves and flat-topped white flower clusters.
Harvest aerial parts (leaves and flowers) during peak bloom.
For immediate field use: crush fresh leaves and press directly onto a clean wound.
For a prepared compress: steep 2 tablespoons dried yarrow in 1 cup boiling water for 15 minutes.
Strain the infusion and let cool to a warm (not hot) temperature.
Soak a clean cloth in the yarrow infusion.
Apply to the wound and hold in place with gentle pressure.
Replace compress every 30 minutes until bleeding slows.
remedies.safety_notes: For minor wounds only -- seek medical attention for deep or serious wounds. Avoid if allergic to Asteraceae family. May increase skin sensitivity to sunlight. Not for use during pregnancy as it may stimulate uterine contractions. Do not use on infected wounds.

remedies.name: Stinging Nettle Allergy Tea
preparation_steps: Wear thick gloves to harvest young nettle tops in early spring (top 4-6 inches).
Rinse thoroughly -- cooking or drying neutralizes the sting.
To dry: spread on screens in a warm, ventilated area for 1-2 weeks.
Use 1-2 tablespoons dried nettle per cup of water.
Pour boiling water over nettle in a cup or teapot.
Cover and steep for 10-15 minutes for a standard tea (or 4-8 hours for a strong nourishing infusion).
Strain and drink. Can add honey, lemon, or mint for flavor.
Drink 2-3 cups daily during allergy season for best results.
remedies.safety_notes: Always wear gloves when handling fresh nettle. May interact with blood thinners, blood pressure medications, and diuretics. Not recommended during pregnancy. Start with small amounts to assess tolerance.

remedies.name: Comfrey Bruise Salve
preparation_steps: Harvest comfrey leaves in summer when the plant is in full growth.
Wash and dry leaves thoroughly (any moisture will cause mold in the oil).
Chop dried leaves and fill a jar halfway.
Cover with olive oil, leaving 1 inch of headspace.
Infuse for 4-6 weeks in a warm spot, shaking every few days.
Strain through cheesecloth, squeezing out all oil.
In a double boiler, melt 1 ounce beeswax per cup of infused oil.
Stir combined mixture and pour into tins. Let cool completely. Apply to unbroken skin over bruises and sore areas.
remedies.safety_notes: FOR EXTERNAL USE ONLY -- comfrey contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids that are toxic to the liver if ingested. Never apply to broken skin or open wounds, as compounds can be absorbed. Limit use to 10 days at a time. Not for use during pregnancy.

remedies.name: Mullein Respiratory Tea
preparation_steps: Identify mullein by its large, soft, fuzzy gray-green leaves and tall flower stalk.
Harvest leaves during the first year of growth (before the flower stalk appears) for best potency.
Dry leaves on screens or hang in bundles for 1-2 weeks.
Use 1-2 tablespoons dried leaf per cup of water.
Pour boiling water over the mullein leaf.
Cover and steep for 10-15 minutes.
IMPORTANT: Strain through a fine cloth or coffee filter to remove tiny leaf hairs (they can irritate the throat).
Drink 2-3 cups daily for respiratory support. Add honey for sore throat relief.
remedies.safety_notes: Always strain thoroughly to remove fine hairs. Generally considered very safe. Mullein seeds are mildly toxic -- use only leaves and flowers. May interact with diuretic medications.
remedies.season: first-year leaves best in summer, year-round (dried)

remedies.name: Sage Sore Throat Gargle
preparation_steps: Harvest sage leaves before or during early flowering.
Use 2 tablespoons fresh sage leaves (or 1 tablespoon dried) per cup.
Pour boiling water over the sage.
Cover and steep for 15 minutes (longer steeping extracts more tannins for astringent effect).
Strain and let cool to a comfortably warm temperature.
Add 1/2 teaspoon salt and optionally 1 teaspoon apple cider vinegar.
Gargle for 30 seconds, spit, and repeat until the cup is empty.
Use 3-4 times daily at the first sign of sore throat.
remedies.safety_notes: For gargling only -- do not swallow large amounts. Sage contains thujone which can be toxic in large doses if ingested. Not recommended for prolonged internal use during pregnancy. Safe for occasional gargling in most adults.

remedies.name: Turmeric Golden Paste
preparation_steps: Combine 1/2 cup turmeric powder with 1 cup water in a saucepan.
Add 1/3 cup coconut oil or olive oil (fat helps absorption of curcumin).
Add 1-1/2 teaspoons freshly ground black pepper (piperine increases curcumin absorption by 2000%).
Stir over low heat for 7-10 minutes until a thick paste forms.
Let cool completely.
Transfer to a clean glass jar and store in the refrigerator.
Use 1/2 to 1 teaspoon daily mixed into warm milk (golden milk), smoothies, or food.
Can also be applied topically as an anti-inflammatory paste for sore joints.
remedies.safety_notes: Turmeric stains everything yellow -- protect clothing and surfaces. May interact with blood thinners, diabetes medications, and stomach acid reducers. High doses may cause digestive upset. Consult a healthcare provider if you have gallbladder issues or are pregnant.

remedies.name: Fresh Aloe Vera Gel
preparation_steps: Select a thick, mature outer leaf from a healthy aloe plant (at least 3 years old).
Cut the leaf at the base with a clean, sharp knife.
Stand the leaf upright in a cup for 10-15 minutes to let the yellow latex (aloin) drain out.
Lay the leaf flat and slice off the spiny edges.
Carefully fillet the leaf by sliding a knife between the gel and the outer skin.
Scoop out the clear gel with a spoon.
Blend briefly for a smooth consistency if desired.
Apply directly to minor burns, sunburn, or irritated skin. Store extra gel in the refrigerator.
remedies.safety_notes: For external use only unless using food-grade inner gel. The yellow latex between skin and gel is a strong laxative -- drain it fully before use. Do not apply to deep wounds or severe burns. Some people are allergic -- do a patch test first. Do not use on surgical wounds.
remedies.yield: varies by leaf size, roughly 2-4 tablespoons per leaf

remedies.name: Willow Bark Pain Relief Tea
preparation_steps: Harvest willow bark in spring when the sap is flowing and bark peels easily.
Select branches 2-3 inches in diameter from a healthy tree.
Score the bark lengthwise and peel it off in strips.
Scrape off the inner bark (cambium layer) -- this is the medicinal part.
Dry bark strips in a warm, ventilated area for 1-2 weeks.
Chop or grind dried bark into small pieces.
Simmer 1-2 teaspoons of dried bark in 1 cup of water for 10-15 minutes (decoction method).
Strain and drink up to 3 cups daily for pain relief. Tastes bitter -- honey helps.
remedies.safety_notes: Contains salicin (similar to aspirin). Do NOT give to children or teenagers (Reye's syndrome risk). Avoid if allergic to aspirin. May interact with blood thinners, NSAIDs, and seizure medications. Not recommended during pregnancy or while breastfeeding. Consult a doctor if taking other medications.
